question_text: What is the sum of all interior angles of a quadrilateral?
teaching_note: Entry check. Students choosing A think quadrilateral angles sum like triangle (180°). Students choosing D double the triangle sum correctly.

options.diagnostic_note: Believes the sum is 180° — applies the triangle angle sum rule to quadrilaterals.
Arbitrary incorrect value — no clear misconception pattern, may be guessing.

options.explanation: A quadrilateral can be divided into 2 triangles, each with 180°. Sum = 2 × 180° = 360°.

options.diagnostic_note: Gives the sum for a pentagon (5 sides). Has the polygon angle sum formula but applied to wrong polygon.

question_text: Three angles of a quadrilateral are 80°, 90°, and 110°. What is the fourth angle?
teaching_note: Applying angle sum = 360°. Students choosing A use 180° as total.

options.diagnostic_note: Subtracted from 180°: 180 − 80 − 90 − 110 = −100°. Used triangle angle sum instead of 360°.

options.explanation: Sum of all 4 angles = 360°. Fourth angle = 360 − 80 − 90 − 110 = 80°.

options.diagnostic_note: Used sum = 380° or made arithmetic error: 80+90+110 = 270, then 380 − 270 = 110. Incorrect total.
Assumed the fourth angle must be 90° because 'it looks like a rectangle'. Applied rectangular right-angle property to a general quadrilateral.

question_text: Which of the following is true for a parallelogram?
teaching_note: Tests basic parallelogram properties. Students choosing B confuse parallelogram with rectangle.

options.diagnostic_note: Thinks all parallelograms have equal sides. Confuses parallelogram with rhombus.
Thinks all parallelograms are rectangles. Over-applies rectangle properties to all quadrilaterals.

options.option_text: Opposite sides are equal and parallel
options.explanation: Defining property: opposite sides of a parallelogram are equal in length and parallel to each other.

options.diagnostic_note: Thinks parallelograms have equal diagonals — this is a property of rectangles, not all parallelograms.

question_text: In parallelogram ABCD, angle A = 70°. What is angle C?
teaching_note: Opposite angles in parallelogram are equal. Students choosing B use co-interior angle property.

options.diagnostic_note: Used 180° − 70° = 110°. Applied co-interior (same-side) angle rule, which applies to adjacent angles, not opposite.

options.explanation: In a parallelogram, opposite angles are equal. Angle A = Angle C = 70°.

options.diagnostic_note: Assumed C must be 90°. Overgeneralised the rectangle to all parallelograms.
Doubled 70° giving 140°. Incorrectly applied a doubling rule with no geometric basis.

question_text: Which quadrilateral has all four sides equal but angles are not necessarily 90°?
teaching_note: Classification of rhombus vs square. Students choosing B confuse square with rhombus.

options.diagnostic_note: Thinks rectangle has all equal sides. Confuses equal-sides property with right-angle property.
Knows square has all sides equal but doesn't know rhombus also does. Thinks square is the only such quadrilateral.

options.explanation: A rhombus has all four sides equal but its angles need not be 90°. A square is a special rhombus with 90° angles.

options.diagnostic_note: Chose trapezium. Has no clear understanding of trapezium's defining property (only one pair of parallel sides).

question_text: A rectangle is a special type of which shape?
teaching_note: Tests understanding of the shape hierarchy. Students choosing D don't know parallelogram definition.

options.diagnostic_note: Thinks rectangle is a type of rhombus. Confuses equal sides (rhombus) with right angles (rectangle).
Thinks rectangle is a type of trapezium. Shows no understanding of the quadrilateral hierarchy.

options.explanation: A rectangle is a parallelogram with all angles equal to 90°. It satisfies all properties of a parallelogram.

options.option_text: None — it is its own shape
options.diagnostic_note: Thinks rectangle is unique and not related to other quadrilateral types. Cannot see the hierarchical relationship.

question_text: In parallelogram PQRS, the diagonals PR and QS bisect each other at O. If PO = 5 cm, what is PR?
teaching_note: Diagonals of parallelogram bisect each other. Students choosing A think PO = PR.

options.diagnostic_note: Thinks PO equals PR. Does not understand that O is the midpoint so PR = 2 × PO.

options.explanation: Diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other, so OR = PO = 5 cm. PR = PO + OR = 5 + 5 = 10 cm.

options.diagnostic_note: Halved PO further. Thinks O divides diagonal into quarters or that PO = half of half of PR.

options.option_text: Cannot be determined without knowing QS
options.diagnostic_note: Thinks diagonals of a parallelogram are equal (like a rectangle), so needs QS too. Confuses diagonal bisection with equal diagonals.

question_text: Which of the following is NOT a property of a rhombus?
teaching_note: Tests ability to distinguish rhombus properties from rectangle/square. Students choosing D think all rhombuses are squares.

options.diagnostic_note: Thinks equal sides is NOT a property of rhombus. Fundamental misidentification.

options.option_text: Diagonals bisect each other at right angles
options.diagnostic_note: Thinks perpendicular diagonal bisection is not a rhombus property. This IS a property of a rhombus.

options.explanation: A rhombus does NOT need to have 90° angles. Only a square (a special rhombus) has all angles equal to 90°.

options.diagnostic_note: Thinks opposite angles equal is not a rhombus property. This IS a property (inherited from parallelogram).

question_text: In a trapezium ABCD, AB ∥ CD. Angles A and D are on the same side as leg AD. If angle A = 65°, what is angle D?
teaching_note: Co-interior angles between parallel lines are supplementary. Students choosing A think they are equal.

options.diagnostic_note: Thinks co-interior angles are equal. Confuses them with alternate angles (which are equal) or vertically opposite angles.

options.explanation: Angles A and D are co-interior angles (same-side interior) between parallel lines AB ∥ CD. They are supplementary: A + D = 180°. D = 180° − 65° = 115°.

options.diagnostic_note: Assumed angle must be 90°. Over-applied rectangle properties to trapezium.
Doubled 65° = 130°. No geometric justification — arithmetic manipulation without understanding.

question_text: A square has a diagonal of length 10 cm. What is the length of each side? (Use √2 ≈ 1.41)
teaching_note: Diagonal of square = side × √2. Students choosing D confuse diagonal with side directly.

options.diagnostic_note: Set side = diagonal. Thinks diagonal and side are equal in length.
Halved the diagonal: 10 ÷ 2 = 5. Confused diagonal bisection (half-diagonal = 5) with the full side length.
Multiplied 10 × 1.41 = 14.1. Used diagonal × √2 instead of diagonal ÷ √2. Inverted the formula.

question_text: A field is in the shape of a parallelogram with base 20 m and height 12 m. What is its area?
teaching_note: Area = base × height. Students choosing A use a side instead of perpendicular height.

options.explanation: Area of parallelogram = base × height = 20 × 12 = 240 m².

options.diagnostic_note: Computed perimeter-like value: 20 + 12 + 20 + 12 = 64. Confused perimeter with area.
Computed (base × height) / 2 = 120. Applied triangle area formula to parallelogram.
Computed 20² = 400. Assumed it was a square and squared the base.

question_text: ABCD is a rhombus with diagonals AC = 16 cm and BD = 12 cm. What is the area of the rhombus?
teaching_note: Area of rhombus = (d1 × d2) / 2. Students choosing C use base × height incorrectly.

options.diagnostic_note: Computed 16 × 12 = 192 without halving. Used product of diagonals without the ÷ 2.
Added diagonals and doubled: (16 + 12) × 2 = 56. Used perimeter-type calculation instead of area.
Used half-diagonals: (8 × 6) × (1/2) = 24 × 2 = 48. Confusion between using full and half diagonals.

question_text: In parallelogram ABCD, angle A = 3x and angle B = 2x + 10. Find x.
teaching_note: Adjacent angles in parallelogram are supplementary. Students choosing B equate the two angles.

options.explanation: Adjacent angles in parallelogram are supplementary: 3x + 2x + 10 = 180. 5x = 170. x = 34.

options.diagnostic_note: Set angles equal: 3x = 2x + 10 → x = 10. Treated adjacent angles as equal (opposite angle property applied wrongly).
Set 3x = 2x + 10 → x = 10. Equated angles as if they were alternate/opposite, not supplementary.
Used sum = 360° for both angles: 3x + 2x + 10 = 360 → 5x = 350 → x = 70 then divided by something. Wrong total used.

question_text: The area of a trapezium is 120 cm². Its parallel sides are 10 cm and 14 cm. What is its height?
teaching_note: Area of trapezium = ½ × (sum of parallel sides) × h. Students choosing B skip the ½.

options.diagnostic_note: Used wrong formula: 120 = (10 + 14) × h → h = 120/24 = 5. Forgot the ½ in the trapezium area formula.
Divided area by one parallel side: 120/10 = 12. Used only one parallel side instead of average of both.
Used 120 ÷ (10 + 4 + 14 + ?) — mixed up perimeter components with area formula.

question_text: Is every square a rectangle? Is every rectangle a square? Choose the correct answer.
teaching_note: Tests hierarchy understanding. A square satisfies all rectangle conditions but not vice versa.

options.option_text: Yes to both — squares and rectangles are the same shape
options.diagnostic_note: Treats square and rectangle as identical. Cannot distinguish between the two despite different side length conditions.

options.option_text: Every square is a rectangle, but not every rectangle is a square
options.explanation: A square has all properties of a rectangle (4 right angles, opposite sides equal) PLUS equal adjacent sides. A rectangle need not have all sides equal.

options.option_text: Every rectangle is a square, but not every square is a rectangle
options.diagnostic_note: Reverses the hierarchy. Thinks the more general shape (rectangle) is a special case of the more specific (square).

options.option_text: Neither — squares and rectangles are completely different quadrilaterals
options.diagnostic_note: Cannot see any relationship between square and rectangle. Treats each shape as isolated.
